coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence quenching biomimetic ELISA based on quantum dots for enrofloxacin detection in animal-derived foods
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Gaoshuang
Liu, Ziyang
Zhang, Yiqin
He, Kuo
Gao, Shan
Hao, Jianxiong
coredata.dc:description: In this study, two novel fluorescence quenching biomimetic enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays were constructed for the sensitive detection of enrofloxacin (ENRO) in animal-derived foods. In this strategy, the fluorescence of quantum dots (QDs) was efficiently quenched by the enzymatic formation of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and Prussian blue nanoparticles (PBNPs) in biomimetic enzyme-linked immunosorbent (BELISA). Under the optimal conditions, the detection limit (IC15) of the method using HRP was 1.67 ng/L, and the sensitivity (IC50) was 0.57 μg L−1, while IC15 of the method using PBNPs reached to 1.02 ng/L, and IC50 was 0.04 μg L−1. The recoveries of these proposed methods ranged from 83.37 to 118.08 %, with the CV values of 0.32–17.01 %. The results from the proposed methods showed high agreement with results yielded by using HPLC. In a conclusion, this conversion of the absorption signal into fluorescent signals led to an increase in the sensitivity of ENRO detection in animal-derived foods. And it is worth noting that compared to the proposed fluorescence quenching BELISA using HRP as catalyst, the method using PBNPs as catalyst showed a higher sensitivity with a 14-fold lower IC50, demonstrating the good application prospect of nano-enzyme in biomimetic immunoassay.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Fluorescence quenching
Biomimetic enzyme-linked immunosorbent
Enrofloxacin
Animal-derived foods

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of enzyme-labelled antigen
Fluorescence quenching BELISA based on QDs
Optimization the conditions for MIP synthesis
Establishment of fluorescence quenching BELISAs based on QDs
Development of fluorescence quenching BELISA with HRP as catalyst
Development of fluorescence quenching BELISA with PBNPs as catalyst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Education of Hebei Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by Science Research Project of Hebei Education Department (BJK2024191).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of fluorescence quenching BELISAs (taking PBNPs as an example).

Characterization of the MIPs. (A) The FT-IR spectra of MIPs, (a) Enrofloxacin, (b) MIPs without elution, (c) MIPs after elution and (d) NIPs. (B) SEM images of NIPs. (C) SEM of MIPs. (D) TGA of MIPs.(E) Adsorption isotherms of the MIPs and NIPs.(F) Kinetic uptake plot of the MIPs.

Fluorescence quenching mechanism. (A) Fluorescence excitation spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of QDs and absorption spectra of TMBox. (B) Zeta potential of QDs and TMBox. (C) Fluorescence recovery of QDs after TMBox ultrafiltration (30 KDa, 4 000 rpm, 5, 10, 15 min). (D) Fluorescence lifetime (τ) of QDs and QDs-TMBox mixture.

Evaluation of the fluorescence quenching effect between the QDs and the enzyme substrates. (A) Changes in fluorescence intensity of QDs at different pH values. (B) Changes of fluorescence intensity of QDs at different H2O2 concentrations. (C) Interference of TMB (2.5 mmol/L) on QDs fluorescence. (D) Changes of fluorescence intensity of quantum dots within 20 min during IFE process. (E) PBNPs, HRP and QDs catalytic substrate TMB.
Standard curves for ENRO of fluorescence quenching BELISA using HRP (red line) and fluorescence quenching BELISA using PBNPs (blue line).
Comparison of the analytical performance of the proposed methods with the other reported methods for ENRO detection in animal-derived foods.

row.entry: Crucian, perch, pork, beef, chicken, milk
Low cost and rapid detection time; narrow detection range and lower sensitivity
Low cost and simple operation steps; higher LOD
Water, milk, honey, beef, pork, shrimp, fish, chicken, egg, milk powder, honey tea, and honey drinks
Analyte extraction → clarifying using water with pH = 11 → solid phase extraction (the hydroxyl-containing triazine-based conjugated microporous polymers) → analysis
High sensitivity; expensive instrument; tedious and time-consuming retreatment
Analyte extraction → repeated membrane filtration → analysis
High sensitivity; complex aptamer preparation and tedious operation steps
Simple operation steps and high selectivity; complex and long-term antibody preparation
Low cost, simple operation steps with considerable sensitivity
Low cost and simple operation steps with high sensitivity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: IC50 and cross-reactivity values for 7 fluoroquinolones analogies with the developed fluorescence quenching BELISA using PBNPs (RSD, n = 3).
Recovery of the spiked ENRO in animal-derived foods detected by these two developed methods and HPLC.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Ps: a Intra-day: averages of three replicates on the same day; b Inter-day precisions: averages of the different three-day replicate experiments.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence quenching biomimetic ELISA based on quantum dots for enrofloxacin detection in animal-derived foods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Biology, Hebei University of Science and Technology, Shijiazhuang, Hebei 050018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Biology
Hebei University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Biology, Hebei University of Science and Technology, Shijiazhuang, Hebei 050018, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shijiazhuang Customs District P.R. China, Shijiazhuang, Hebei 050061, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shijiazhuang Customs District P.R. China, Shijiazhuang, Hebei 050061, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hebei North University, Zhangjiakou, Hebei 075000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hebei North University, Zhangjiakou, Hebei 075000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, two novel fluorescence quenching biomimetic enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays were constructed for the sensitive detection of enrofloxacin (ENRO) in animal-derived foods. In this strategy, the fluorescence of quantum dots (QDs) was efficiently quenched by the enzymatic formation of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and Prussian blue nanoparticles (PBNPs) in biomimetic enzyme-linked immunosorbent (BELISA). Under the optimal conditions, the detection limit (IC15) of the method using HRP was 1.67 ng/L, and the sensitivity (IC50) was 0.57 μg L−1, while IC15 of the method using PBNPs reached to 1.02 ng/L, and IC50 was 0.04 μg L−1. The recoveries of these proposed methods ranged from 83.37 to 118.08 %, with the CV values of 0.32–17.01 %. The results from the proposed methods showed high agreement with results yielded by using HPLC. In a conclusion, this conversion of the absorption signal into fluorescent signals led to an increase in the sensitivity of ENRO detection in animal-derived foods. And it is worth noting that compared to the proposed fluorescence quenching BELISA using HRP as catalyst, the method using PBNPs as catalyst showed a higher sensitivity with a 14-fold lower IC50, demonstrating the good application prospect of nano-enzyme in biomimetic immunoassay.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Enrofloxacin (ENRO) belongs to the fluoroquinolones class of antibiotics [1], it kills bacteria by blocking DNA replication and repair and is designated by the state as an animal-specific drug [2]. However, due to the unreasonable use of ENRO by breeders, residual phenomena occurred in animal-derived foods. Then serious side effects occur when consumers had contaminated foods. China has strictly controlled the application of ENRO to edible animals, and specified the amount of drug residues in food, limiting the common meat residue value to 100 μg kg−1 [3]. The European Union has set the maximum residue limit of ENRO in the muscle of aquatic products at 100 g kg−1, even some countries, such as the United States and Canada, do not allow the use of quinolones in aquaculture [4,5].

At present, the commonly used detection methods for ENRO include high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6] and ultra-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS) [7–9]. Although these methods have the advantages of high separation efficiency and accurate quantification, they need expensive instruments and cumbersome pretreatment procedures, and their wide application in the field of food safety detection is limited. Due to the advantages of high sensitivity and fast detection speed, immunoassay has been widely used for ENRO detection [10,11]. However, antibody used in these methods has poor physical and chemical stability and requires sacrifice of animals.

Molecular imprinted technology (MIT) is a molecular recognition technology capable of generating molecular holes with specific recognition templates by the use of chemical synthesis means [12,13], it shows advantages of low cost, high specificity and high sensitivity [14]. Combined MIT with enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), the biomimetic ELISA (BELISA) showed the advantages of being straightforward, convenient, and animal cruelty-free [2]. However, the conventional BELISA still suffer from lower sensitivity because of the lower signal to noise (S/N) ratio, making it unsuitable for detecting the trace levels of chemical contaminants in foodstuffs. Fluorescence nanoparticles, including quantum dots (QDs), have been reported to be used in the above strategy to replace absorption signals [15]. However, these methods suffer from cumbersome and complicated fabrication of fluorescence nanoparticles-antibody, and these methods sacrifice the signal amplification of enzymatic reactions, usually resulting in no significant increase in sensitivity compared to that of BELISA. Fluorescence quenching effect has attracted much attention in recent years, it refers to the phenomenon that fluorescence molecules lose the excited state, return to the ground state under certain conditions, and the fluorescence is weakened [16,17]. The strategy based on fluorescence quenching effect could make the fabrication of the probe with obvious flexibility and simplicity, so as to improve the sensitivity of the method [18]. Thus, fluorescence quenching BELISA which takes advantage of signal amplification by the use of enzymic catalytic reaction and fluorescence, is more promising and practical in the analysis area. And it is worth noting compared with natural enzymes including HRP used in BELISA, nano-enzyme is more suitable for various biological matrix-related systems in experiments, since nano-enzyme not only combines the physical and chemical properties of natural enzyme and enzyme catalytic activity, but also has the advantages of low preparation cost, easy preparation and convenient transportation [19].

Based on the above views, two novel fluorescence quenching BELISAs were constructed and compared for sensitive ENRO detection in animal-derived foods. MIPs were used as bionic antibody, natural HRP enzyme and PBNPs nano-enzyme were used to catalyze substrate TMB respectively, and then the resulting oxidation product TMBox could fluorescence quenching QDs and trigger fluorescence quenching effect, converting the colorimetric signal in traditional BELISA into fluorescence signal, resulting in higher sensitivity for ENRO detection (as shown in Fig. 1 ). The proposed methods provide alternative choice for efficient and sensitive detection of ENROs in animal-derived foods.

Enrofloxacin (ENRO, purity > 99 %) purchased from Shanghai Jiuding Chemical Technology Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China), α-methacrylic acid (MAA, purity ≥ 98 %), purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China), Ice acetic acid (AR), purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., LTD. (Tianjin, China), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, AR) purchased from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. (Tianjin, China), Bovine Serum albumin (BSA, purity ≥ 98 %), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, purity > 99 %), horseradish peroxidase (HRP, purity > 99 %), N, N-dicyclohexyl carbodiimide (DCCI, purity > 99 %), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, purity > 99 %) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China), ZnCdSe/ZnS quantum dots (QDs, emission wavelength was 525 nm under the excitation of 405 nm), purchased from Wuhan Jiayuan Quantum Dots Co., LTD. (Wuhan, China). The concentration of QDs is 8 μmol/L, and the particle size is 4–10 nm. Bioland 8-well removable polystyrene label plate (96-well microplate, transparent, 200–300 ng/cm2, medium binding force, F-bottom, no RNase/DNase, no heat source), purchased from Zhejiang Bioland Biotechnology Co., LTD. (Zhejiang, China).

Shimadzu UV-2300 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was employed to obtain ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra. Fourier infrared spectrum was obtained with a Great 20 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Tianjin Zhongke Ruijie Technology Co, LTD, Tianjin, China); SEM images were obtained using SEM field emission scanning electron microscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Fluorescence lifetime change was determined by using fluorescence lifetime spectrometer LifeSpec II (Tianmei Yituo Laboratory Equipment Co, LTD, Shanghai, China).

The procedural steps for the synthesis of MIPs are as follows: 360 mg ENRO (1 mmol) was mixed with 8 mL of methanol/toluene mixture (3:1, v/v), and ultrasonic for 10 min. 340 μL MAA (4 mmol) was added into the above mixture and incubated at room temperature for 2 h, and then 308 μL TRIM (1 mmol) was added and reacted for 30 min. Subsequently, 50 mg AIBN was added to the pre-polymerization solution. After a 5-minute ultrasonic degassing process, 96-well microplate containing 50 μL of the pre-polymerization solution was purged with nitrogen for 5 min and reacted at 45 °C for 12 h. Following the completion of the polymerization procedure, MIPs were eluted for 8 h using a methanol/glacial acetic acid mixture (9/1, v/v), and was subsequently rinsed for 4 h using 800 mL of methanol. Eventually, MIPs were produced at room temperature through natural drying.
Using the same experimental procedure, a nonimprinted polymer (NIP) was fabricated for comparison, however, ENRO was not added.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of enzyme-labelled antigen

ce:section.ce:para: ENRO conjugate was prepared by the traditional activated ester method [20] 8.90 mg ENRO was dissolved into 150 μL N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). 3.50 mg N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 12.40 mg N,N-dicyclohexyl carbodiimide (DCCI) were added to the above solution. Then the mixture was gently stirred at room temperature for 8 h, and the precipitation produced in the mixed solution was removed by centrifugation. Under the condition of the ice bath, the supernatant of the activated ester solution was slowly added into the solution of sodium bicarbonate dissolved with 10 mg HRP enzyme (3 mL, 130 mmol/L, pH 8.1), and then stirred overnight at 4 °C, and then the reaction solution was transferred to a dialysis bag. Dialysis was carried out at 4 °C with phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (0.01 mol/L, pH 7.4) for 3 days. Finally, the obtained ENRO-HRP was characterized using UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer. It can be seen that the maximum absorbance for ENRO-HRP gave a significant peak shift compared with the maximum absorbance for ENRO at 280 nm, verifying the successful synthesis of ENRO-HRP (Fig. S1).

Prussian blue nanoparticles (PBNPs) were prepared according to the reported literature with slight modifications [21] 10 mmol/L citric acid was added to 20 mL 20 mmol/L FeCl3 aqueous solution, and then 20 mL 20 mmol/L K4[Fe(CN)6] aqueous solution containing the same amount of citric acid was added to the solution. After stirring at 60 °C for 1 min, the solution was cooled to room temperature and continued stirring at room temperature for 15 min. An equal volume of acetone was then added to the dispersion. The PBNPs were then obtained by centrifugation at 10 000 rpm for 15 min.

Then silylation modified PBNPs was prepared to increase its biocompatibility. 200 mg of PBNPs was added to 100 mL of water (pH 6.74) and 1 mL of 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane to prepare solution A. 10 μL 25 % ammonia was diluted to 1 mL to prepare solution B. Under the condition of the ice bath, solution B is slowly added to solution A, so that the pH of solution A is 8.0, and the hydrolysis of silane coupling agent is catalyzed. The reaction was stirred for 12 h, and then centrifuged at 10 000 rpm for 10 min to remove the reaction liquid. Subsequently, ethanol was added to wash the obtained silylation modified PBNPs for three times. The SEM image and grain-size diagram of the obtained particles are shown in Fig. S2. The SEM showed that the surface of PBNPs was rough, its particle size was uniform, the range was 100 ∼ 150 nm and the shape was similar to spherical. The particle size of PBNPs was 204.71 nm and the Zeta potential was −49.30 mV as determined by the DLS. According to literature reports, the surface modification of nanoparticles with citric acid can prevent them from agglomerating and have good dispersion in water [22,23]. Therefore, citric acid is added to modify the surface of PBNPs during the preparation process, hydrophilia of PBNPs is improved to prevent further growth and agglomeration of nanoparticles, thus achieving protection and stability.

ENRO-OVA was synthesized referred Hiroo’s work [24], and the prepared ENRO-OVA was shown in Fig. S3. Then ENRO-OVA-PBNPs was prepared as the following procedure, 10 mg PBNPs were dissolved in 2 mL 0.01 mol/L PBS buffer solution, 8 mg EDC and 16 mg sulfo-NHS were added and reacted for 30 min at 37 °C. 200 uL ENRO-OVA was added into the above solution and the mixture were gently shaken at 37 °C for 2 h. After centrifugation at 10 000 rpm and 4 °C for 15 min, the conjugation was then reacted with 2 mL 10 % BSA for 2 h at room temperature to block the unbound ENRO-OVA site on the surface of PBNPs. Finally, the ENRO-OVA-PBNPs was obtained by centrifuging at 10 000 rpm for 15 min and stored at 4 °C with 200 μL 1 % BSA. It can be seen that the maximum absorbance (740 nm) for ENRO-OVA-PBNPs gave a slight peak shift compared with the maximum absorbance for PBNPs at 720 nm, verifying the successful synthesis of ENRO-OVA-PBNPs (as shown in Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching BELISA based on QDs
ce:section.ce:para:
These two fluorescence quenching BELISAs were developed using the following protocol: 100 μL of the ENRO standard solutions (sample solution) and an equal volume of the ENRO-HRP/ ENRO-OVA-PBNPs were added in each well. The mixture was then allowed to react for 1 h at 37 °C. After washed with PBST for five times, the plate was incubated for 10 min with 100 μL of the mixed tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) substrate and 20 μL QDs. Ultimately, measurement of the fluorescence intensity was carried out at the excitation/ emission wavelength at 405/ 525 nm, and the inhibition rate was calculated using the following expression:
(1)
I n h i b i t i o n
r a t e
( % )
=
1 -
F
Blank
- F
F
Blank
-
F 0
× 100 %
Where Fblank, denotes the fluorescent signals of the blank well (enzyme label and ENRO standard solution are not added), F0 corresponds to the fluorescence intensity of wells enzyme marker is added, without ENRO, and F stands for the fluorescence intensity of the ENRO-spiked wells.

As a bionic antibody of ENRO, the selectivity of MIPs was investigated by the cross-reactivity (CR) of ENRO to its structural analogues, including enoxacin (EX), norfloxacin (NOR), ciprofloxacin (CIP), ofloxacin (OFX), pefloxacin (PEF), marbofloxacin (MBF) and gatifloxacin (GEM). Additionally, the calibration curves were constructed. The equation was used to compute the cross-reactivity (CR):
(2)
C R % =
I C
50
( E N R O )
/
I C
50
( o t h e r
a n a l o g i e s )
× 100
Where IC50 (ENRO), is the corresponding concentration of ENRO at the 50 % inhibition rate, and IC50 (other analogies), is the corresponding concentration of other analogies at the 50 % inhibition rate.

Animal-derived foods (confirmed negative samples by HPLC) such as milk, chicken, shrimp, and eggs were chosen as testing samples from local supermarkets to assess the viability of the suggested approach. For chicken, shrimp and egg samples, 5 g homogenized specimen was placed in a 50 mL centrifuge tube, and 5 mL of 50 % methanol-PBS extraction liquid (pH 5.7) was added and swirled for 5 min. Following a 15-minute ultrasonic treatment to completely extract the target from the sample, the mixture was subjected to centrifugation for 5 min at 12 000 rpm. At last, the supernatant was diluted to an appropriate concentration using PBS (pH 5.7). The milk sample was directly detected after being diluted with 50 % methanol-PBS at the proper ratio.

The samples not containing ENRO were spiked using 1, 10, and 100 μg L−1 of ENRO. Then, the suggested assays, and HPLC analysis were used to examine the spiked samples concurrently (the specific procedures for the HPLC analysis have been given in the accompanying Supplementary material).
All experiments were conducted three times, and the results were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Data were analyzed by Variance Analysis software Excel 2019 and Origin 64.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization the conditions for MIP synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal ratios of ENRO template molecules, MAA functional monomers, and TRIM cross-linkers were utilized in the MIP preparation process to guarantee that the resultant polymer had a good adsorption capacity. Fig. S5 displays the different ratios and adsorption capacities that were investigated during the work. The appropriate ratio of methanol:toluene (pore-making agent) was 3:1 (v:v), the appropriate dosage of pore-making agent was 8 mL, and ENRO:MAA:TRIM ratio was 1:4:1. The most suitable adsorption medium was PBS with 20 % methanol, and the polymer showed better performance with degassing treatment. At these conditions, the polymer showed the highest adsorption capacity. These optimized conditions were subsequently employed in the course of experiments.

Fig. 2 A compares the FT-IR spectra of the NIPs (d), the MIPs prior to elution (b), the MIPs following elution (c) and ENRO (a). As evident, the ENRO stretching peak is located 3452 cm−1, 1737 cm−1 and 1627 cm−1, suggesting O-H, the C=O stretch of the 4-pyridone and carboxyl group. The hydrophobic interaction between the –OH group of MAA and the C=O group of ENRO for the MIPs prior to eluting is responsible for the stretch shift in the position of spectra (a). Characteristic peaks representing the symmetric and asymmetric vibration of the ester's C-O generated by MAA-TRIM crosslinking were also found at 1264 cm−1 and 1155 cm−1. The characteristic peaks of 2821 cm−1, 1255 cm−1 and 1463 cm−1 in spectra (a) are the expansion vibration of –CH2-, shear vibration and C-F in the ENRO structure, respectively. Due to the binding of ENRO and MAA, the characteristic peak shows a stretch shift and overlap in spectra (b). These findings proved that ENRO and MAA underwent a chemical reaction. According to the FT-IR spectra, the main bands of the eluted MIPs and the NIPs had comparable appearance and locations indicating that the ENRO had been eliminated from the MIPs during organic elution. These outcomes proved that the MIPs preparation were successful.

Morphology of MIPs and NIPs was observed by SEM. The SEM images revealed that MIPs (Fig. 2C) of ENRO present more cavities with different sizes and different shapes, larger surface area in comparison with that of NIPs (Fig. 2B). The smaller pore size of the MIPs could be beneficial to high adsorption capacity, potentially facilitating easy access to the enzyme conjugate.

The MIPs's thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curve is displayed in Fig. 2D. The mass of the MIPs did not significantly decrease as the temperature increased from 0 to 400 °C; the primary cause of the minimal mass loss at this stage is the loss of water from the MIPs's surface. The mass reduced drastically when the temperature rose beyond 400 °C. This is because the MIPs thermally degrade. In a conclusion, MIPs exhibited good thermal stability, according to the results.

To evaluate the binding ability and adsorption of the MIPs, 200 μL different concentration ENRO standard solution was added to both the MIPs and NIPs (60–160 mg L−1). The following expression was used to calculate the adsorption capacity (Q) of the MIPs and NIPs:
(3)
Q = ( C -
C 1
) V /
M
where C0 and C1 are the concentrations of ENRO in the solution before and after adsorption, respectively, M is the mass of the MIPs or NIPs and V is the volume of the solution.

As shown in Fig. 2E, the adsorption capacity of the MIPs and NIPs for ENRO adsorption underwent an increase as the concentration of ENRO increased, and the adsorption capacity tended to reach saturation when the concentration was above 160 mg L−1. The reason for this adsorption change is determined by the characteristics of the polymer itself. At the beginning of adsorption, the template molecule rapidly binds to the imprinting site on the surface of the polymer. When all the imprinting sites on the surface are combined, the template molecule needs to enter the carrier and bind to the imprinting site for adsorption, thus slowing down the adsorption efficiency. Due to the large specific surface area of the polymer and the large porous structure, the diffusion of ENRO inside the polymer presents a nonlinear enhancement. However, when the adsorption capacity reaches the extreme value, the increase of the ENRO standard concentration no longer has a significant impact on the adsorption capacity, and the adsorption of ENRO by MIPs and NIPs reaches the saturation adsorption capacity [25]. When the concentration was 160 mg L−1, the adsorption capacity of MIPs reached 5.35 μg well−1), which was 6.15 times that of NIPs (0.87 μg well−1). The adsorption capacity of ENRO-MIPs is obviously higher than that of NIPs, indicating that MIPs have strong specific adsorption to ENRO.

The adsorption kinetics of the MIPs was also investigated in this study. As shown in Fig. 2F, the adsorption capacity reached 53.12 % of the maximum value when incubated for 20 min, and the adsorption equilibrium was reached at 60 min. The results indicated that MIPs have a good adsorption efficiency on ENRO, and the adsorption kinetics of the MIPs can meet the need for efficient detection of ENRO in the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of fluorescence quenching BELISAs based on QDs

ce:section.ce:para: Two completely novel fluorescence quenching BELISAs designed for the quick identification of ENRO have been developed in this investigation. Higher sensitivity for ENRO detection was achieved by efficiently quenching the fluorescence of QDs through the enzymatic synthesis (TMBox) of HRP and PBNPs in BELISAs. This process also converted the absorption signal into fluorescent signals. A mechanism of fluorescence quenching between QDs and TMBox was assessed.

As shown in Fig. 3 A, the maximum absorption of TMBox at 370 nm overlaps the excitation wavelength of QDs at 410 nm, thus we suspect that an effective fluorescence inner filter effect (IFE) would occur between TMBox and QDs, and the excitation light of QDs would be absorbed by TMBox because of its high molar absorption coefficient according to previous study [26]. It is worth noting that the absorption peak of TMBox at 640 nm also overlaps the maximum emission wavelength of QDs at 520 nm to a small extent. The potential of QDs is −53.70 mV, indicating that QDs has a negative charge, while the potential of TMBox is 2.70 mV, indicating that it has a positive charge (as shown in Fig. 3B). Electrostatic interactions between positively charged TMBox and negatively charged QDs shorten the distance between these two substances, thus we speculate that there maybe weak fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between TMBox and QDs. To further confirm the existence of FRET, fluorescence quenching mechanism was distinguished by measuring whether fluorescence lifetime attenuation occurred before and after QDs reaction with TMBox. It can be seen from Fig. 3D that τ was 3.38 ns before the reaction of QDs and τ was 2.58 ns after quenching by TMBox, the fluorescence lifetime of QDs decreased by 23.67 % compared with that of QDs. The results show that there is a dynamic quenching between QDs and TMBox. In addition, we separated QDs from the quenching system by ultrafiltration test and determined the fluorescence intensity of QDs to verify whether the fluorescence of QDs can be restored [27]. As shown in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence intensity of QDs recovered with increasing time from 0 min to 15 min, indicating that no non-fluorescent complex was formed, further confirming the dynamic quenching mechanism. Therefore, we can conclude that the fluorescence quenching effect produced is not only caused by a single IFE but also caused by FRET between QDs and TMBox in the experiment.

Effective fluorescence quenching is contingent upon the fluorescence stability of quantum dots (QDs) during measurement [28]. As previously mentioned, oxidants and pH can affect QDs' fluorescence signals [29], etc. The HRP substrate (pH 4.5) additionally contains H2O2 (10 mmol/L) and TMB (2.5 mmol/L). The fluorescence stability of QDs in the matrix was methodically evaluated to prevent the signal interference brought by the detecting system matrix (rather than the TMBox). As shown in Fig. 4 A, the results show that the fluorescence stability of QDs was good in the range of pH 4.0 to 8.0, while the optimal pH value of HRP activity is mostly around 7.0, and the synthesis environment of PBNPs is around pH 7.4. The effect of oxidizer (H2O2) concentration on the fluorescence intensity of QDs was also evaluated. The addition of H2O2 did not influence the fluorescence of QDs until the concentration of H2O2 rose above 100 mmol/L, which was significantly more than the concentration of H2O2 in the substrate, as seen in Fig. 4B. As shown in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence intensity of QDs had no change after mixed with TMB, indicating that TMB has no obvious interference on fluorescence of QDs. The fluorescence stability of QDs during IFE was verified by adding QDs to deionized water (DDW), TMB + H2O2, and TMBox, the fluorescence of QDs is very stable for 20 min in this process, as shown in Fig. 4D, which indicates that QDs has excellent light stability. The enzyme properties of QDs were also evaluated. The OD values of HRP, PBNPs, and QDs catalytic substrate TMB were determined. As shown in Fig. 4E, OD values of HRP and PBNPs substrate increased with the increase of time, while QDs did not change significantly, indicating that QDs had no catalytic ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of fluorescence quenching BELISA with HRP as catalyst
ce:section.ce:para:
To achieve the highest sensitivity of the proposed methods, the dilute solution, the dilution ratio of QDs and the dilution ratio of HRP-labeled ENRO were optimized. The ideal buffer for fluorescence quenching BELISAs with HRP as catalyst was 20 % carbinol-PBS (pH 5.7), the ideal dilution ratio for QDs was 1:8 000, and the ideal dilution ratio for ENRO-HRP was 1: 75. The parameters of the method were evaluated under the optimal conditions, sensitivity was evaluated using the IC50, which is defined as the concentration of target giving a 50 % inhibition. Also, the detection limits (LOD) was calculated as IC15achieve the highest sensitivity of the proposed [20]. Fig. 5 shows that the linear regression equation of the method is y = 5.9984ln(x) + 53.344 (R2 = 0.9748), IC15 and IC50 of this method were 1.67 ng/L and 0.57 μg L −
1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of fluorescence quenching BELISA with PBNPs as catalyst
ce:section.ce:para:
The ideal buffer for fluorescence quenching BELISA with PBNPs as catalyst was PBS (pH 5.7), the ideal dilution ratio for QDs was 1:4 000, and the ideal dilution ratio for ENRO-OVA-PBNPs was 1:600. The linear regression equation of the fluorescence quenching BELISAs using the PBNPs was y = 9.3807 ln(x) + 79.319 (R2 = 0.9992), and the IC15 was 1.02 ng/L and IC50 was 0.04 μg L −
1.

Compared with that of the method constructed by using HRP, the method using PBNPs showed higher sensitivity and lower detection limit, the IC50 and IC15 values of the method using PBNPs were reduced by 92.98 % and 38.92 %, respectively. It may be caused by the fact that fluorescence quenching BELISAs with PBNPs as catalyst showed a more efficient fluorescence quenching effect due to the better catalytic performance of nano-enzyme. In addition, PBNPs nano-enzyme shows advantages of stable properties, low preparation cost and easy storage (Figs. S6-S7). Also, the performance of these two developed methods was compared with the previous reports for the detection of ENRO [15,20,30–32]. As shown in Table 1 , these two proposed methods showed advantages of lower cost, higher sensitivity, wider linearity range and simple pretreatment, which could be efficient methods for the detection of ENRO in animal-derived foods.

Selectivity of the proposed method was determined by evaluating the reactivity of the MIPs with other analogies, and because we use the same MIPs, the selectivity was studied using fluorescence quenching BELISA using PBNPs as the catalyst since it showed high sensitivity. As shown in Table 2 , the proposed method detected 9.63 % and 5.94 % cross-reactivity (CR) with EX and NOR, respectively. Relative to CIP, OFX, PEF, MBF and GEM, the cross-reactivity ranged from 0.79 % to 1.54 %. Results showed that MIPs had good specificity and can be used for detection of ENRO.

The detection of the bionic antibody and targets may be impacted by a variety of chemical substances present in the samples, including protein and fat. These will lower the assay's reliability and sensitivity. Diluting the extract with the standard solution and comparing the standard curve with a calibration curve developed in the sample matrix was one effective technique to eliminate the matrix effect of the samples. The matrix effect is negligible if the two curves are stacked. In this investigation, we discovered that the samples were diluted five times with PBS, the calibration curves produced for the chicken and milk samples were superimposed with the standard curve, and this allowed the matrix interferences to be reduced (Fig. S8A-B). On the other hand, the egg samples were diluted ten times using PBS and the shrimp samples were diluted twenty times with PBS could similarly reduce matrix interference (Fig. S8C-D).

To assess the precision and suitability of the two procedures, samples of chicken, eggs, shrimp, and milk that had been spiked with ENRO at varying concentrations (1, 10, and 100 μg kg−1/μg L−1) were examined and compared by HPLC and these two procedures. As shown in Table 3 , the recoveries of ENRO ranged from 83.37 to 118.08 %, with coefficients of variation of 0.32 to 17.01 %, indicating good accuracy and precision of this method. Meanwhile, the established methods showed high agreement with HPLC, indicating that the proposed methods had good practicability. As a conclusion, the proposed methods could be employed for sensitive quantitative detection of ENRO in animal-derived foods.

In this study, two novel fluorescence quenching BELISAs, using HRP and PBNPs as the catalyst, were constructed for the sensitive detection of ENRO in animal-derived foods. Compared with that of the method constructed by using HRP, the method using PBNPs showed higher sensitivity (0.04 μg L−1), lower detection limit (1.02 ng/L) and higher stability due to the application of nano-enzyme (PBNPs) with better catalytic performance and stable properties. Under 5-fold dilution in chicken and milk, 10-fold dilution in egg and 20-fold dilution in shrimp, acceptable recoveries from spiked samples suggested that these two proposed BELISA method are reasonable quantitative/screening methods in animal-derived foods. As a conclusion, these two proposed methods were reliable and relatively economic, and could be alternative choice for the efficient detection of ENRO in animal-derived foods. However, it is worth noted that the experimental steps of the present developed methods are relatively complicated, some more simple and time-saving method, including test strips based on similar principle of this study, can be further expanded in the future.
This work was funded by Science Research Project of Hebei Education Department (BJK2024191).
This paper does not contain any studies or tests performed on human participants or animals.
Informed consent was obtained from all individual participants included in the study.

Gaoshuang Hu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Ziyang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation. Yiqin Zhang: Supervision, Resources, Investigation. Kuo He: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology. Shan Gao: Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Jianxiong Hao: Formal analysis, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data (Fig. S1. UV-vis absorption spectrum of ENRO, HRP and ENRO-HRP by UV-vis absorption spectrophotometer. Fig. S2. The SEM image and grain-size diagram of PBNPs. Fig. S3. UV-vis absorption spectrum of ENRO, OVA and ENRO-OVA by UV-vis absorption spectrophotometer. Fig. S4. UV-vis absorption spectrum of ENRO-OVA and ENRO-OVA-PBNPs by UV-vis absorption spectrophotometer. Fig. S5. Optimization of synthesis conditions of ENRO MIPs. (A) Selection of pore-causing agents. (B) Selection of the total amount of pore-causing agent. (C) Optimization of ENRO: MAA molar ratio. (D) Optimization of the types of crosslinkers. (E) ENRO: MAA: TRIM molar ratio optimization. (F) Determination of degassing conditions. (G) Selection of adsorption media. (H) Optimization of pH value of adsorption medium. (I) Optimization of methanol content. Fig. S6. The catalytic activities of HRP and PBNPs at different PH. Fig. S7. The catalytic activities of HRP and PBNPs temperature. Fig. S8. The comparisons of different dilution ratio with PBS to minimize the matrix effects of chicken (A), milk (B), egg (C), shrimp (D) sample.) to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110589.

ce:section.ce:para:

(D) Optimization of the types of crosslinkers. (E) ENRO: MAA: TRIM molar ratio optimization. (F) Determination of degassing conditions. (G) Selection of adsorption media. The catalytic activities of HRP and PBNPs temperature. Fig. S8. The comparisons of different dilution ratio with PBS to minimize the matrix effects of chicken (A), milk (B), egg (C), shrimp (D) sample.
